title: The Crew: The Quest for Planet Nine

subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: Exact counts from the provided rulebook text: 40 large playing cards total (36 color cards in 4 colors, values 1-9, plus 4 rocket cards values 1-4), 36 small task cards, 5 large reminder cards, 16 tokens total (10 task tokens, 5 radio communication tokens, 1 distress signal token), 1 commander token, and 1 rulebook/logbook. Mission play also references the mission logbook (50 missions) and uses task cards plus communication tokens as core systems each mission attempt.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Before each mission attempt: shuffle all 40 large playing cards and deal them face down as evenly as possible to all crew members. If 3 players are playing, one person receives one extra card; after the last trick, one card remains unplayed. Each player places one radio communication token green-side up in front of themselves. Keep one reminder card ready per player. Place distress signal token face down. Shuffle all 36 task cards and place them as a facedown pile.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: Read the mission to determine how many task cards are used. Reveal that many task cards face up in the center. The player holding Rocket 4 is commander and takes commander token. Task draft starts with commander and proceeds clockwise, one task card at a time, until all revealed tasks are assigned (players may receive different numbers of tasks). Only after tasks are distributed may players communicate using radio tokens. Commander leads the first trick unless mission text says otherwise.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
Mission flow in order:
1. Mission Preparation: deal large cards, set communication tokens, prepare task cards/tokens, determine commander (Rocket 4).
2. Task Distribution: reveal required task cards and draft clockwise starting with commander.
3. Trick Play Loop:
   a) Leader plays first card of trick (sets suit).
   b) In clockwise order, each other player plays one card.
   c) Follow-suit required if possible.
   d) Highest card in led suit wins trick unless rockets are played; highest rocket wins.
   e) Trick winner leads next trick.
4. Mission Check: complete all assigned tasks to win; if any required card is won by wrong player or a mission rule is broken, mission fails immediately.
5. On failure, reshuffle and restart mission with new deal/tasks.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
On your play in a trick, you must play exactly one card. Legal card play is determined by follow-suit: if you have the led suit, you must play that suit; if not, you may play any card. You are never forced to play your highest eligible card and are never required to try to win the trick. Rockets are trump cards: they win against color cards; if multiple rockets are in one trick, highest rocket wins. If a rocket is led, rockets become the suit for that trick and must be followed if possible.

Communication actions (strictly limited): each player may communicate exactly once per mission attempt, and only before a trick (never during a trick). To communicate, place one non-rocket color card face up and place your token to indicate: top = highest card of that color in your hand, middle = only card of that color, bottom = lowest card of that color. One of these must be true at the moment of communication. Rocket cards cannot be communicated. Token position cannot be changed later even if the statement would no longer be true. After communicating, take a reminder card; when that communicated card is played, return reminder card and flip communication token red.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content: Task ownership is the core mission mechanic: each small task card corresponds to one specific playing card that must be captured by that task owner. Tasks are fulfilled when owner wins a trick containing the matching playing card; fulfilled tasks are turned face down. A player may complete multiple tasks in one trick if matching cards are captured. The game is cooperative, but hidden information is tightly constrained: players may not show, suggest, or discuss hand contents beyond permitted communication token actions. Won tricks are placed face down; only the most recently won trick may be inspected. Mission structure is campaign-based across 50 missions, with difficulty and symbols introducing additional constraints later in the logbook.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
The Crew has no victory points. Mission outcome is binary:
- Mission success: all assigned task cards are fulfilled by their correct owners under any mission-specific rules.
- Mission failure: immediate if any required playing card is won by a player who does not own that task, or if a mission special rule is violated.
After success, proceed to the next mission and optionally log number of attempts. After failure, reshuffle all cards, redeal, redistribute tasks, and retry the same mission. There are no tie-breakers because missions are pass/fail cooperative objectives, not comparative scores.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: 1) Before first trick, identify where your task cards are likely to appear and which suits you can safely control. 2) If you are commander, choose first task that matches your strongest certainty (high card control in that color or rocket protection). 3) Use your one communication only when it resolves a real branching decision (who should lead, who should dump, who must avoid winning). 4) In early tricks, establish lead order intentionally; who wins now determines who controls suit selection next trick. 5) Preserve rockets for mission-critical saves rather than routine trick wins.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content: 1) Communicating too early with low-impact info; you then lack communication when mission pressure peaks. 2) Treating rockets like guaranteed wins and burning them before critical task cards appear. 3) Forgetting that wrong-player capture is instant loss, leading to casual overtricking. 4) Ignoring leader control: winning an unnecessary trick can force you to lead a dangerous suit. 5) Breaking communication rules by hinting from private-hand knowledge outside the token system.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content: A) Safety vs tempo: play high now to secure a known task, or play low to preserve control tools for later tasks. B) Communication timing: spend your once-per-mission signal early to coordinate setup, or hold it for a high-risk endgame trick. C) Leader management: decide when to intentionally lose a trick so the right teammate can choose the next suit and route target cards correctly.

metadata.notes: Written from provided The Crew rulebook excerpt (materials, trick rules, communication, mission prep/sequence).
